description: Credibility / bias rules orthogonal to tier. Used by Phase 4.5 Credibility Triage + Phase 7 verification + Phase 8 gate. Bias class is separate from tier (a Tier-3 peer-reviewed paper can be DECLARED-COI; a Tier-4 grey source can be NEUTRAL).

bias_classes.NEUTRAL: Regulator (incl. authoritative EU bodies EFSA/ECHA), academic, public research institute, non-affiliated researcher
bias_classes.POLITICAL: Advocacy NGO with declared anti or pro-PPP agenda
bias_classes.CAVEAT: Authoritative body with recognised methodological / framework / reputation caveats (e.g. IARC). Cite-with-pairing rule applies — never alone on the contested topic; always paired with relevant regulatory consensus and divergence noted.
bias_classes.PREDATORY: Beall/Cabells listed publisher; pay-to-publish without rigorous peer review
bias_classes.TRADE_PRESS: Industry trade outlets; market/factual context only
bias_classes.UNRELIABLE: Wikipedia, blogs, social media, anonymous web sources

political_domains.description: Domains classified as POLITICAL. Cite-replace rule applies — never standalone evidence in CITATION-LEDGER.md.
political_domains.rationale: These organisations are designed to influence regulatory and public policy. Their selection bias, framing, and synthesis decisions are value-laden. Cite the upstream Tier 1-3 source directly; drop the advocacy framing.

predatory_block.description: Hard-block publishers. Beall's archived list + Cabells subset relevant to chemistry/tox/regulatory.

grey_listed.description: Case-by-case publishers; downweight Tier 3 to Tier 3- with peer-review-rigour flag. Cite only if independent corroboration exists.

MDPI.notes: Large legitimate corpus; rapid review cycle weakens RoB. Prefer for replication/triangulation, not as sole source on contested endpoints.

Frontiers.notes: Rapid peer review; flag for contested endpoints.

Hindawi.default_action: case-by-case post-Wiley cleanup; check for retraction flags
Hindawi.notes: Legacy issues; verify journal currently active and indexed in Web of Science / Scopus.

Bentham Science.default_action: accept (distinct from legacy Bentham Open which is blocked)
Bentham Science.notes: Some legitimate journals; flag if author affiliations are predominantly opaque.

unreliable_block.description: Block for citation. Permitted for discovery / synonym lookup only.
categories: press releases (cite the underlying study, not the release)

trade_press_domains.description: Permitted for market / factual industry context only. BLOCK if used for scientific claim.

authoritative_status_note.description: EFSA and ECHA are recognised as authoritative for EU PPP regulatory work regardless of occasional political pressure. They are the EU regulatory framework. Tier 1 NEUTRAL status maintained. Where their conclusions diverge from each other or from other bodies, document the divergence; do not downgrade either.

caveat_sources.description: Bodies that are citable and accessible, but carry recognised methodological / framework / reputation caveats. Cite-with-pairing rule applies: never alone on the contested topic; always paired with the relevant regulatory consensus source(s) and the divergence noted explicitly.

iarc.rationale: IARC operates under a hazard-identification-only framework (Preamble to the Monographs, 2019). Uses publicly available literature only — excludes unpublished registrant studies that form the basis of EFSA / ECHA / EPA assessments. Group classifications (1, 2A, 2B, 3) are hazard categories, not risk statements. Has documented reputation for working-group composition disputes (glyphosate 2015), selective evidence inclusion (mechanistic data weighting), and divergence from regulatory consensus where the full dataset is available. Treat IARC findings as scientifically interesting hazard signals — not as regulatory conclusions.
cite_protocol: Always paired with relevant EU regulatory consensus (EFSA Conclusion, ECHA RAC opinion) when cited.
Explicit divergence statement required when IARC classification differs from EU classification.
Hazard-vs-risk distinction explicitly preserved: IARC Group 2A/2B/1 is hazard identification, not risk under registered GAP.
Note framework limitation: 'IARC assessment based on publicly available literature; EU assessment includes full regulatory dataset including registrant studies' or equivalent.
Never cite IARC alone on carcinogenicity claims.
Never use IARC classification as proof of human carcinogenicity at exposure-relevant doses without explicit MoE / exposure context.
applicable_substances_recent: glyphosate (2A, 2015 — diverges from EFSA 2023, ECHA RAC 2022)

caveat_sources.future_additions: Other bodies meeting the caveat profile (hazard-only framework + reputation for divergence from regulatory consensus + selective literature base) may be added here. Examples worth monitoring: certain NTP technical reports where IARC-style framework applied; certain advocacy-aligned academic review groups.

contested_endpoints.description: Endpoints with high political loading or active regulatory dispute. Triangulation rule applies: ≥1 Tier 1-2 source AND ≥1 independent peer-reviewed Tier 3 corroboration required. Single-source claims downgrade hedging.
list: cocktail effects / mixture toxicity beyond CAG
contested_endpoints.rule: ≥1 Tier 1-2 + ≥1 independent peer-reviewed Tier 3 corroboration. Independent = different first-author, different funder, different institution. Single-source = hedging downgrade + [single-source] tag in ledger.

hazard_vs_risk_guard.description: Phrases / claim patterns that conflate hazard classification with risk under registered GAP. Flag at Phase 6 and Phase 8 Step 7.
forbidden_unguarded: is an endocrine disruptor in humans
hazard_vs_risk_guard.rule: These claims require explicit exposure context (PEC, MoE, dietary intake, IESTI) in the same sentence or adjacent citation. Without exposure context, reframe to maintain hazard-vs-risk distinction or drop. CLP / IARC classifications are hazard statements, not risk statements.

rules: If candidate claim's only citation trail involves POLITICAL or TRADE_PRESS sources without independent Tier 1-3 corroboration → drop claim.
If ledger row has coi=advocacy or coi=trade_press, require ≥1 other ledger row with tier ≤2 supporting same claim_summary.
Patterns to detect: NGO-A → NGO-B → NGO-A (circular); NGO → trade-press → NGO (advocacy-trade loop); NGO → blog → NGO (advocacy-blog loop).

claim_reframing_examples.description: Examples of how to reframe POLITICAL-source claims when an upstream Tier 1-3 source exists.

examples.political_framing: PAN Europe: 'Glyphosate is a probable carcinogen banned by science'
examples.upstream_primary: IARC Monograph Vol 112 (2017): Group 2A 'probably carcinogenic to humans' based on limited human evidence and sufficient animal evidence
examples.regulatory_consensus: EFSA Conclusion 2023 + ECHA RAC 2022: no CLP carcinogen classification warranted based on full dataset including registrant studies
examples.review_text: EFSA peer review (2023) and ECHA RAC opinion (2022) concluded no CLP carcinogen classification warranted for glyphosate based on the full regulatory dataset including registrant studies [CIT-EFSA-2023, CIT-ECHA-2022]. IARC (2015, Monograph Vol 112) classified glyphosate as Group 2A based on publicly available literature only [CIT-IARC-2015]. The divergence reflects different assessment frameworks: IARC operates a hazard-identification framework using public-domain literature; EU regulatory peer review applies a hazard identification + risk characterisation framework using the full dataset, including unpublished registrant studies. IARC's Group 2A is a hazard category, not a statement of risk under registered GAP.
examples.notes: Regulatory consensus (EFSA + ECHA) cited first; IARC cited paired with explicit framework-divergence note per caveat_sources protocol; hazard-vs-risk distinction explicitly stated.

examples.upstream_primary: USDA Pesticide Data Program annual report — residue detections, all below tolerances
examples.regulatory_context: EU MRLs Reg 396/2005; EFSA Annual Monitoring Report
examples.review_text: USDA PDP (2024) detected residues of [substances] in [N]% of strawberry samples [CIT-USDA-2024]; all detections were below US tolerances. EU monitoring (EFSA, 2024) reported [N]% MRL exceedance rate for strawberries from EU production [CIT-EFSA-MR-2024].
